content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 28, 2007

Charles H. Bell, Jr.
Bell McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-180

Dear Mr. Bell:

This letter responds to your inquiry regarding the committee information provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We provide informal assistance because you
ask a general question of law, on which you have a duty to advise your clients, that is not
related to a specific set of facts or circumstances on which we might offer formal advice.²

QUESTION

You ask that we clarify or reconcile a seeming inconsistency in the Commission's
historical interpretation of the time at which a person may become a recipient committee
by operation of the "one bite of the apple" rule stated at Regulation 18215(b)(1).

CONCLUSION

The Commission has consistently advised that after an organization makes its first
political contributions or expenditures totaling $1,000 or more, the presumption stated at
Regulation 18215(b)(1) – that the organization's donors do not have reason to know that
their subsequent payments would be used to make contributions or expenditures – is
negated. Thus if contributions or expenditures of $1,000 or more are again made by the
organization during the current calendar year or any of the four following years, the
presumption does not shield the organization from qualification as a recipient committee

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the immunity conferred by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).) (Copy enclosed.)

File No. I-07-180
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

Regulation 18215 interprets the definition of the term "contribution" given at
Section 82015. In particular, Regulation 18215(b) provides:

"(b) The term 'contribution' includes:

(1) Any payment made to a person or organization other than a
candidate or committee, when, at the time of making the payment, the
donor knows or has reason to know that the payment, or funds with which
the payment will be commingled, will be used to make contributions or
expenditures. If the donor knows or has reason to know that only part of
the payment will be used to make contributions or expenditures, the
payment shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to determine
the amount of the contribution.

There shall be a presumption that the donor does not have reason
to know that all or part of the payment will be used to make expenditures
or contributions, unless the person or organization has made expenditures
or contributions of at least one thousand dollars ($1,000) in the aggregate
during the calendar year in which the payment occurs, or any of the
immediately preceding four calendar years.

Your question concerns the italicized language at the end of the second paragraph
of this subdivision. You suggest that in the Olson Advice Letter, No. I-90-515, we inter-
preted this language to mean in effect that the presumption applicable to donors to an
organization that made contributions or expenditures totaling at least $1,000 in January of
a given year would continue to apply to those donors throughout the remainder of the
calendar year, with the result that they would be presumptively ignorant of any additional
contributions or expenditures made by the organization during the course of that year.³

You correctly note a different view of this regulation in the recent Rherig Advice
Letter, No. A-07-126, which followed the Fishburn Advice Letter, No. A-06-075, to a
conclusion quite unlike the one you perceive in the Olson letter. In the Fishburn and
Rherig letters we said that once an organization makes its first political contributions or
expenditures totaling $1,000 or more, the presumption that its donors do not have reason
to know that their subsequent payments would be used to make contributions or
expenditures no longer applies. We explicitly said that "if contributions or expenditures
of $1,000 or more are again made by the organization during the current year or any of
the four following years, the organization becomes a recipient committee...." (Rherig
Advice Letter, emphasis added. See also the Hoffer Advice Letter, No. I-96-280.)

³ The Olson Advice Letter predates the current version of Regulation 18215(b) and, strictly
speaking, was not construing the language of the current regulation, adopted five years after the letter was
written.

File No. I-07-180
Page No. 3


The interpretation presented in the Rherig and Fishburn letters is consistent with
the words of the regulation, quoted above, describing the effect of contributions or
expenditures of $1,000 or more on a subsequent payment of $1,000 or more that are
made "during the calendar year in which the payment occurs."

The Olson letter offers no support for a contrary view. You cite the following
quotation from that letter:

"Once the labor union has established a history of making contributions
from its general fund, its members are deemed to be on notice in subsequent
years that a portion of their payments may be used for political purposes.
Thus, in any subsequent calendar year in which the labor union makes
contributions out of its general fund totaling $1,000 or more, it will qualify
as a recipient committee under Section 82013." (Your emphasis added.)

This passage is an answer to a specific question which may not have been
transcribed quite so clearly in the resulting advice letter. The language you quote
responded to the following question, on page one of the request for advice:

"Would the union be required to register as a recipient committee pursuant
to Government Code Section 82013 if the original contribution was at
least $1,000 and the union made a subsequent contribution the following
calendar year from its general treasury funds?" (Emphasis in original.)

The passage you cite from the Olson letter answered a question about a contri-
bution made by a union in the calendar year following its original contribution of $1,000
or more. The request for advice did not ask about effects on subsequent contributions in
the same calendar year, a subject on which the Olson letter is naturally silent. There is,
therefore, no conflict between the Olson Advice Letter and advice afterwards given in the
Rherig and Fishburn letters on a question neither asked nor answered in the Olson letter.

To the extent that there is any ambiguity at all in the language of the regulation,
Section 81003 provides that "[t]his title should be liberally construed to accomplish its
purposes." The first of the Act's statutory purposes is stated at Section 81002(a),
providing that "[r]eceipts and expenditures in election campaigns should be fully and
truthfully disclosed in order that the voters may be fully informed and improper practices
may be inhibited." This purpose is poorly served by an interpretation of Regulation
18215(b) that presumes donors to an organization remain ignorant of the uses to which
their donations are being put when the organization makes contributions or expenditures
totaling $1,000 or more in January, and continues to do so throughout the year leading up
to a general election in November.

File No. I-07-180
Page No. 4


Finally, The United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit correctly
articulated the operation of the "one bite of the apple" rule in an extensive discussion
based in large part on the Fishburn Advice Letter, in the recently decided California
ProLife Council, Inc. v. Randolph, et al. (November 14, 2007, see Slip Opinion at pages
14815 -14823.) We conclude that the view expounded in the Fishburn Advice Letter is
correct, and is consistent with prior advice on this subject.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LTW:jgl

Enclosure
